mnemonics: PIPR (Program Counter -> Instruction Register -> Processor): Visualize data flowing from the Program Counter (PC), to the Instruction Register (IR), and finally being processed by the CPU's internal components.
FDE (Fetch, Decode, Execute): The fundamental, repeating cycle of all computer operation. Think of it as the computer's heartbeat.
J-BRANCH (Jump, Branch): These two types of instructions are the primary way to change the normal sequential flow (PC+1) of a program.
keyPoints: The Program Counter (PC) holds the memory address of the *next* instruction to be fetched.
The Instruction Register (IR) holds the actual instruction that is currently being executed.
Most instructions execute sequentially (PC = PC + 1). Control flow instructions (Jumps, Branches) change this sequence by loading a new address into the PC.
The instruction format is typically split into an opcode (what to do) and operands (what to do it to).
The Control Unit is the hardware component that generates the signals to orchestrate the Fetch-Decode-Execute cycle.
commonMistakes: Confusing the PC (address of next instruction) with the IR (the current instruction itself).
Forgetting that the PC is incremented *after* the fetch step, not before.
Assuming a branch instruction is always taken; the condition must be evaluated first.
Mixing up the opcode field with the operand fields when interpreting a machine instruction's binary pattern.
